q: Equilibrium means a state of balance.
There are two types of equilibrium: static equilibrium and dynamic equilibrium.
A book at rest on a table is in dynamic equilibrium because the weight of the book is balanced by the normal reaction force from the table.
All chemical reactions are dynamics.
In a sealed bottle of soda, carbon dioxide is present in both the liquid phase and the gaseous phase.
Inside a sealed soda bottle, the two forms of carbon dioxide are in static equilibrium.
In a sealed bottle of soda, gaseous carbon dioxide is turning into a liquid at the same rate that the liquid carbon dioxide is turning back into a gas.
Chemical equilibria are important in numerous biological and environmental processes.
The maintenance of equilibrium in reactions between haemoglobin and oxygen in human blood is one of the processes involving human health.
The carbon cycle contributes to the important equilibrium process that minimises the impacts of carbon dioxide on the environment.
A chemical equilibrium is a dynamic equilibrium where reactants are continuously being changed to products and products are continuously being changed back to reactants.
A dynamic equilibrium exists once an irreversible reaction occurs.
Many chemical reactions go to completion, meaning the products cannot be converted back to reactants.
In a reversible reaction, the forward reaction is where products are converted back into the starting reactants.
The symbol $\rightleftharpoons$ is used to represent the reversible reaction.
In the reverse reaction of $A + B \rightleftharpoons C$, the substance $C$ decomposes into $A$ and $B$.
A reversible reaction is one that can occur in both the forward and reverse directions, where conversion occurs simultaneously.
Reversible reactions that happen in an open system eventually reach equilibrium.
Reversible reactions are in equilibrium when the rates of the forward and reverse reactions balance.
In dynamic equilibrium, the forward and reverse reactions stop once the system reaches a steady state.
Dynamic equilibrium leads to constant concentrations of the reactants and products.
As a reaction proceeds towards equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants gradually increase while products gradually decrease.
After a system has reached equilibrium, the concentrations of all substances remain constant.
The rate of the forward reaction is low and the rate of the reverse reaction is high initially in a reversible process.
Equilibrium is reached when the two rates of the forward and reverse reactions are equal.
The state of chemical equilibrium is dynamic in nature because reactions are still proceeding at an equal rate.
Chemical equilibrium can be classified into homogeneous and heterogeneous chemical equilibria based on the phase of the reactants and products.
In homogeneous chemical equilibrium, the reactants and the products of chemical equilibrium are all in a single phase.
The reaction $N_{2} (g) + O_{2} (g) \rightleftharpoons 2NO (g)$ is an example of heterogeneous chemical equilibrium.
In heterogeneous chemical equilibrium, the reactants and the products are present in more than one phase.
The equilibrium $H_{2}O (l) \rightleftharpoons H_{2}O (g)$ is classified as a heterogeneous chemical equilibrium.
A closed system is a system that allows energy to enter and exit, but matter can neither be gained nor lost.
The reaction $Fe_{2}O_{3} (s) + 3CO (g) \rightleftharpoons 2Fe (s) + 3CO_{2} (g)$ is a homogeneous equilibrium because all species are in the same phase.
Any combination of solid, liquid, or gas states, as well as solutions, can exist in heterogeneous equilibrium.
Le Chatelier's principle is a qualitative guide to the effect of changes in concentration, temperature, and pressure on a system at equilibrium.
French Chemist Henri Louis Le Chatelier discovered his namesake principle in 1884.
If a system is in equilibrium and a factor affecting it is changed, the equilibrium shifts to cancel out the effect of the change.
If a factor affecting the system of chemical equilibrium is changed, the system will respond in the same direction as the change.
By removing something from an equilibrium system, the system will react to replace it.
Once equilibrium is established, further change is apparent only if the external factors are altered.
The greater the stress, the more the direction of the reaction shifts in response to the stress.
Stress always causes a shift in equilibrium, even when the moles of reactants and products are equal after a change in gas pressure.
A catalyst does not affect the equilibrium concentrations and results in no change in equilibrium.
Adding or removing a reactant or product from a reaction mixture at equilibrium will always disturb the equilibrium.
When the concentration of a reactant is increased, the reaction proceeds in the forward direction in favour of products.
When the concentration of a reactant is decreased, the reaction proceeds in the forward direction to reestablish equilibrium.
Increasing the concentration of a product results in the reaction proceeding in the reverse direction in favour of reactants.
When the concentration of a product is decreased, the equilibrium shifts to the right to reestablish equilibrium.
The effect of temperature on chemical equilibrium depends upon the heat changes ($\Delta H$) of the reaction.
If the forward reaction is exothermic, the reverse reaction will also be exothermic.
A temperature decrease favours an exothermic reaction, while a temperature increase favours an endothermic reaction.
In the case of exothermic reactions ($A \rightleftharpoons B$ $\Delta H = -$), decreasing the temperature favours the exothermic reaction by releasing heat.
In endothermic reactions ($A \rightleftharpoons B$ $\Delta H = +$), increasing the temperature shifts equilibrium towards the forward reaction.
The decomposition of $N_{2}O_{4}$ (colourless) to $NO_{2}$ (reddish-brown) has a positive $\Delta H$.
In the equilibrium between $N_{2}O_{4}$ and $NO_{2}$, decreasing the temperature favours the endothermic reaction.
Increasing the temperature of a reaction mixture containing $N_{2}O_{4}$ and $NO_{2}$ causes it to become reddish-brown.
Pressure changes markedly affect the solid or liquid phases in chemical equilibrium.
The pressure of a gas mixture at a given temperature is determined by the number of gas molecules in a given volume.
If the pressure is increased by reducing the volume of the container, the position of equilibrium shifts towards the side with fewer moles of gas.
The side of a gas reaction with fewer moles will occupy the largest volume.
In chemical equilibrium, the change of pressure in both liquids and solids can be ignored because their volumes are independent of pressure.
According to Le Chatelier’s principle, as pressure decreases, the equilibrium moves toward the side of the reaction with more moles of gas.
If the number of moles of gas is the same on both sides of a reversible chemical equation, pressure has no effect on the equilibrium.
In the reaction $N_{2} (g) + 3H_{2} (g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_{3} (g)$, increasing pressure shifts the equilibrium to the right.
For the reaction $H_{2} (g) + I_{2} (g) \rightleftharpoons 2HI (g)$, increasing pressure will shift the equilibrium to the left.
The synthesis of ammonia (Haber process) is an industrial process that uses Le Chatelier’s principle.
The forward reaction in the synthesis of ammonia is endothermic.
To produce the maximum yield of ammonia, the temperature must be maintained at a level that is low enough to continue the forward reaction.
Ammonia synthesis is favoured by high pressure because there are four moles of reactants for every two moles of product formed.
The catalyst used in ammonia production is iron(III) oxide with a small amount of aluminium oxide.
Methanol is produced from carbon monoxide and hydrogen through a reversible reaction.
The formation of methanol is an exothermic reaction.
Increasing the temperature in methanol production will shift the position of equilibrium to the right.
Methanol production is carried out at a temperature of $250 \text{ }^{\circ}C$ using the catalyst $Cu-ZnO-Al_{2}O_{3}$.
According to Le Chatelier's principle, increasing the pressure in methanol synthesis shifts the equilibrium to form more moles of methanol.
When running on a treadmill, the person is in a state of dynamic equilibrium because they move forward at the same rate the treadmill moves backward.
The composition of an equilibrium mixture does not change when a catalyst is added.
For an endothermic reaction, an increase in temperature would favour the reverse reaction.
The law of mass action was proposed by two Norwegian chemists in 1864.
The law of mass action is a quantitative law for predicting the amounts of reactants and products at dynamic equilibrium.
According to the law of mass action, the ratio between reactant and product concentrations is constant only if the temperature is changing.
The rate of a forward reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of the reactants raised to a power equal to their coefficients.
At equilibrium, the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal.
The ratio $\frac{k_{f}}{k_{r}}$ is called the equilibrium constant, $K_{eq}$.
The value of $K_{eq}$ is constant at all temperatures for a specific chemical reaction.
The equilibrium constant provides how far a reaction has gone towards completion before it reaches equilibrium.
Pure solids and liquids are included in the equilibrium expression because their concentrations change significantly.
The concentrations of pure solids or liquids remain constant throughout the reaction.
If the $K_{eq}$ value is large ($K_{eq} >> 1$), the equilibrium lies to the right and there are more products than reactants.
If the $K_{eq}$ value is small ($K_{eq} << 1$), the equilibrium lies to the left and there are more reactants than products.
When the $K_{eq}$ value is equal to one ($K_{eq} = 1$), both reactants and products are equally favoured at equilibrium.
The equilibrium constant $K_{c}$ is used when molar concentrations are involved.
For equilibrium involving gases, partial pressures can be used to express the equilibrium constant $K_{p}$.
Only $K_{p}$ can be applied for gaseous reactions.
The equilibrium constant for a homogeneous equilibrium, like $H_{2} (g) + I_{2} (g) \rightleftharpoons 2HI (g)$, is expressed as $K_{eq} = \frac{[HI]^{2}}{[H_{2}][I_{2}]}$.
For the equilibrium between ethanol (l) and ethanol (g) in a closed flask, the constant expression is $K_{eq} = [C_{2}H_{5}OH (g)]$.
Ethanol is a pure liquid, so its concentration is constant at a given temperature.
The equilibrium for the sublimation of iodine crystals depends only on the concentration of gaseous iodine in the system.
The numerical value of the equilibrium constant is determined experimentally by measuring concentrations at equilibrium.
Equilibrium constant is a ratio that expresses the relationship between the amounts of products and reactants present at equilibrium in a reversible chemical reaction at a given temperature.
The transfer of $O_{2}$ from the lungs to the blood depends on chemical equilibrium between oxygen and haemoglobin.
Reversible reactions can proceed in the forward direction only.
An increase in temperature would favour the exothermic reaction.
The effect of changing the pressure on a reaction involving liquids is negligible.
The equilibrium does not depend on the initial concentration of reactants.
